title: Threat modeling is just asking what could go wrong
hook: Threat modeling sounds formal until you do it. Then it is mostly drawing the system and asking where trust gets weird.

beats.heading: Security reviews fail when they start too late

blocks.html: If the first security conversation happens after the feature is built, every fix feels expensive. A lightweight threat model before implementation can catch the cheap changes.

blocks.title: design · trust boundaries
blocks.caption: The useful signal is usually already there.

lines.t: browser -> API -> queue -> worker -> payment provider

blocks.caption: Security gets real when the trust boundaries light up. Poke it and see what has to hold.

blocks.html: Name the actors, data, trust boundaries, secrets, and worst reasonable outcomes. You do not need a perfect diagram to find the scary edges.

blocks.code:
Asset: payment token
Threat: replay refund request
Control: idempotency key and authorization check
Open question: webhook retry window
blocks.caption: A useful threat model creates concrete engineering questions.

beats.heading: Turn threats into tests or controls

blocks.html: The model is only useful if it changes the system: new validation, logging, authorization, rate limits, or a decision not to build the risky path.

blocks.title: security control lab
blocks.caption: Flip the switches and watch the story turn into a tiny operating model.
blocks.low: exposed: the feature trusts too much and records too little
blocks.mid: better: the easy abuse paths now have friction
blocks.high: hardened: authorization, limits, and recovery all have a job

items.label: things to map: actors, data, boundaries, assets

blocks.html: Threat modeling is not a ceremony. It is a cheap way to avoid discovering the architecture from the breach report.
